content:
# Luke Chapter 3 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 87-88 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_003_page_001.jpg, LUK_003_page_002.jpg

**1** It was the fifteenth year of the reign of Tiberius Caesar. Pontius Pilate governed Judea, Herod ruled Galilee, his brother Philip ruled the region of Iturea and Trachonitis, and Lysanias ruled Abilene.

**2** Annas and Caiaphas served as high priests. During this time, the word of God came to John, Zechariah's son, out in the wilderness.

**3** He went throughout the whole region around the Jordan, calling people to be baptized as a sign that they had turned from their sins and received forgiveness.

**4** This fulfilled what was written in the scroll of the prophet Isaiah: "A voice cries out in the wilderness: 'Prepare the way for the Lord! Make his paths straight!

**5** Every valley will be filled in, every mountain and hill brought low. The crooked roads will be straightened and the rough ways made smooth.

**6** And all humanity will see God's salvation.'"

**7** To the crowds coming out to be baptized by him, John said, "You brood of vipers! Who warned you to flee from the coming wrath?

**8** Produce fruit that proves you have genuinely changed. And don't start telling yourselves, 'Abraham is our father.' I tell you, God can raise up children for Abraham from these very stones!

**9** The axe is already at the root of the trees. Every tree that does not produce good fruit will be cut down and thrown into the fire."

**10** The crowds asked him, "Then what should we do?"

**11** He answered, "If you have two tunics, share with someone who has none. If you have food, do the same."

**12** Even tax collectors came to be baptized and asked him, "Teacher, what should we do?"

**13** He told them, "Don't collect more than what you are authorized to."

**14** Soldiers also asked him, "And what about us — what should we do?" He said, "Don't extort money from anyone or make false accusations. Be content with your wages."

**15** The people were filled with expectation, and everyone was wondering in their hearts whether John might be the Messiah.

**16** John answered them all: "I baptize you with water, but someone more powerful than I is coming. I am not worthy even to untie the strap of his sandals. He will baptize you with the Holy Spirit and with fire.

**17** His winnowing fork is in his hand. He will thoroughly clean out his threshing floor, gathering the wheat into his storehouse — but the chaff he will burn with fire that can never be quenched."

**18** With many other urgent appeals, he proclaimed the good news to the people.

**19** But when John rebuked Herod the tetrarch for marrying Herodias, his brother's wife, and for all the other evil things Herod had done,

**20** Herod added this to all the rest: he locked John up in prison.

**21** Now when all the people were being baptized, Jesus too was baptized. And as he was praying, heaven was torn open,

**22** and the Holy Spirit descended on him in a bodily form like a dove. And a voice came from heaven: "You are my Son, the Beloved. In you I am well pleased."

**23** Jesus was about thirty years old when he began his ministry. He was the son (so it was thought) of Joseph, son of Heli,

**24** son of Matthat, son of Levi, son of Melchi, son of Jannai, son of Joseph,

**25** son of Mattathias, son of Amos, son of Nahum, son of Esli, son of Naggai,

**26** son of Maath, son of Mattathias, son of Semein, son of Josech, son of Joda,

**27** son of Joanan, son of Rhesa, son of Zerubbabel, son of Shealtiel, son of Neri,

**28** son of Melchi, son of Addi, son of Cosam, son of Elmadam, son of Er,

**29** son of Joshua, son of Eliezer, son of Jorim, son of Matthat, son of Levi,

**30** son of Simeon, son of Judah, son of Joseph, son of Jonam, son of Eliakim,

**31** son of Melea, son of Menna, son of Mattatha, son of Nathan, son of David,

**32** son of Jesse, son of Obed, son of Boaz, son of Sala, son of Nahshon,

**33** son of Amminadab, son of Admin, son of Arni, son of Hezron, son of Perez, son of Judah,

**34** son of Jacob, son of Isaac, son of Abraham, son of Terah, son of Nahor,

**35** son of Serug, son of Reu, son of Peleg, son of Eber, son of Shelah,

**36** son of Cainan, son of Arphaxad, son of Shem, son of Noah, son of Lamech,

**37** son of Methuselah, son of Enoch, son of Jared, son of Mahalaleel, son of Cainan,

**38** son of Enosh, son of Seth, son of Adam, son of God.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 1 — Historical anchoring:** Luke alone among the Gospel writers sets the scene with this elaborate political framework — five rulers named — grounding the story in verifiable world history. This is not myth; it is journalism.

**Verse 2 — "The word of God came to John":** The Greek phrasing (egeneto rhema theou epi Ioannen) echoes the Old Testament prophetic call formula (cf. Jeremiah 1:1, Hosea 1:1). Luke is deliberately presenting John as the last in the line of Israel's prophets.

**Verse 3 — Baptism of repentance:** The Sinaiticus reads "baptism of repentance for forgiveness of sins" (baptisma metanoias eis aphesin hamartion). "Repentance" (metanoia) means far more than regret — it is a complete turning around, a reorientation of one's entire life.

**Verse 4 — Isaiah quotation:** Luke extends the Isaiah 40 quotation further than Matthew or Mark, continuing through verse 6 to include "all flesh shall see the salvation of God." This is theologically deliberate — Luke's universal vision insists that God's rescue reaches every nation, not Israel alone.

**Verse 7 — "Brood of vipers":** In Matthew, this rebuke targets the Pharisees and Sadducees specifically. In Sinaiticus Luke, John addresses the crowds broadly — everyone is implicated, not just the religious elite.

**Verse 8 — "From these stones":** A devastating wordplay in the original. The Aramaic for "children" (benaiya) sounds nearly identical to "stones" (abnaiya). God's promise to Abraham is not a family heirloom to be claimed by bloodline; it is a living power that can create a people from nothing.

**Verses 10-14 — Three groups ask "What should we do?":** This is unique to Luke. The crowds, the tax collectors, and the soldiers each receive concrete, practical answers — not abstract theology but immediate economic justice: share clothing, stop corruption, end extortion. John's vision of repentance is deeply material.

**Verse 16 — "Holy Spirit and fire":** Sinaiticus preserves the standard reading here. The dual baptism — Spirit and fire — may represent purification and judgment, or empowerment and refining. The image is of total transformation, not gentle improvement.

**Verse 17 — Winnowing fork:** The threshing-floor image would have been viscerally familiar to an agrarian audience. The winnower tosses grain into the wind; the heavy wheat falls back to the floor while the light chaff blows away. It is a picture of inescapable sorting.

**Verse 20 — Herod's response:** Luke compresses the Herod-John conflict into two devastating verses and finishes with the imprisonment — before narrating Jesus' baptism. This creates the striking effect that John is already imprisoned when Jesus' ministry begins, emphasizing the transfer of prophetic authority.

**Verse 22 — "You are my Son, the Beloved":** Sinaiticus reads su ei ho huios mou ho agapetos, en soi eudokesa — "You are my Son, the Beloved; in you I am well pleased." Some early manuscripts (notably Codex Bezae) read instead "Today I have begotten you" (quoting Psalm 2:7), but Sinaiticus supports the standard reading. The voice from heaven combines Psalm 2:7 and Isaiah 42:1, fusing the royal Messiah with the Suffering Servant.

**Verse 23 — "So it was thought":** The parenthetical hos enomizeto ("as was supposed") signals Luke's awareness of the virgin birth tradition while still presenting the legal genealogy through Joseph.

**Verses 23-38 — The genealogy:** Luke's genealogy runs backward from Jesus to Adam to God — the reverse of Matthew's, which runs from Abraham to Jesus. This is theologically loaded: Matthew traces Jesus to Abraham (Israel's father), but Luke traces him all the way back to God through Adam. Every human being shares that lineage. Jesus belongs not merely to Israel but to the entire human race.

**Verse 33 — "Admin, son of Arni":** Sinaiticus preserves the distinctive readings Admein and Arni in the genealogy, where other manuscripts read Amminadab son of Aram. These variant spellings reflect the fluidity of transliterated Hebrew names in early Greek manuscripts.

**Verse 36 — Cainan:** The inclusion of Cainan (Kainam) between Arphaxad and Shelah follows the Septuagint tradition (Genesis 10:24 LXX) rather than the Hebrew Masoretic text, which omits this generation. Sinaiticus preserves this reading, confirming Luke's reliance on the Greek Old Testament.

**Verse 38 — "Son of God":** The genealogy's final link — Adam, son of God — is breathtaking in its simplicity. It means that the story of Jesus is not a footnote to Jewish history but the culmination of all human history, from the very first breath of creation.
